title: Degressive Democracy: Agent-Based Simulation of Irreversible Vote Withdrawal as a Democratic Accountability Mechanism
description:
Degressive Democracy is an agent-based simulation of a proposed democratic accountability mechanism: citizens can withdraw their vote from an elected politician once per legislative term, irreversibly. This creates continuous accountability pressure independent of the next election cycle.

Findings: Promise-keeping is a Nash equilibrium under the mechanism. Populists are eliminated in every simulated configuration. At municipal level, the mere existence of the mechanism suffices as deterrence; no withdrawals are needed in equilibrium. The simulation models six citizen archetypes, five politician strategies, and four power models, with a Prospect Theory satisfaction model and Germany-specific scenarios.

Methodology: 249 deterministic unit tests, 21 simulation modules, formal Nash proof in concepts/nash_proof.md, sensitivity tornado analysis. Interactive HTML dashboard for exploring the parameter space.

Open source under Apache 2.0.
notes: Single-author research artifact. 249 deterministic unit tests. Includes formal Nash equilibrium proof and an interactive dashboard for parameter exploration.
